_comment: Concept catalog for teach-and-check. Each card: kidName (taught/checked), realName (bonus mention only — never required from young kids), teach (one concrete sentence), check (default either/or, said-out-loud friendly), reteach (a DIFFERENT analogy), hint (smallest nudge), coAnswer (the guaranteed-win say-it-together line), mustHaveIdea (core meaning for judging the open upgrade), olderCheck (optional open question for ~11+), acceptExamples (messy-but-correct replies that should PASS — for judge calibration). Extend freely; keep the shape.

cards.teach: A memory box keeps your high scores safe even after you turn the game off.
cards.check: Does the memory box remember your scores, or forget them?
cards.reteach: Think of your toy box: you close the lid and your toys are still inside. So when the game turns off, does the memory box KEEP your scores or LOSE them?
cards.hint: Tiny hint — it KEEPS your stuff. So… keep or lose?
cards.coAnswer: Say it with me: the memory box KEEPS my scores! Boom — you got it.
cards.mustHaveIdea: stores/keeps information so it survives after the game closes
cards.olderCheck: What's the memory box doing for your game?

cards.teach: A remember-number is like a sticky note that holds one number for you — like how many lives you have.
cards.check: Does it hold ONE number, or ALL the pictures in the game?
cards.reteach: Picture a tiny whiteboard on the wall with your lives written on it. You can rub it out and write a new number. Does that whiteboard hold one number, or a whole pile of stuff?
cards.hint: Hint — it's just one number at a time, like your lives going 3… 2… 1.
cards.coAnswer: Together: it holds ONE number! Like your lives. Nice.
cards.mustHaveIdea: holds a single value that can change
cards.olderCheck: What does your lives counter remember, and how does it change?

cards.teach: Your score is the number that goes UP every time you do something good, like grabbing a coin.
cards.check: Does the score go UP or DOWN when you grab a coin?
cards.reteach: Every coin is like a point in a game of tag-you're-it. More coins = more points. So grabbing a coin makes the number go which way — up or down?
cards.hint: Hint — good stuff makes it grow!
cards.coAnswer: Together: the score goes UP! Grab more coins, bigger score.
cards.mustHaveIdea: a number that increases as you earn points
cards.olderCheck: What makes your score change, and when?

cards.teach: A do-it-again tells the computer: do this thing over and over! That's how coins keep falling.
cards.check: Does a do-it-again do something ONCE, or AGAIN AND AGAIN?
cards.reteach: It's like singing 'row, row, row your boat' — you sing the same line over and over. Does a loop do its thing one time, or keep repeating?
cards.hint: Hint — it repeats, like a song stuck on replay.
cards.coAnswer: Together: again and again! That's a loop.
cards.olderCheck: What's the loop doing to make the coins keep falling?

cards.teach: A rule says: IF you touch the spike, THEN you lose a life. The game follows your rules!
cards.check: Does the rule happen ALWAYS, or ONLY WHEN you touch the spike?
cards.reteach: It's like 'if it rains, then you grab an umbrella.' You only grab it when it actually rains. So the spike rule runs always, or only when you touch the spike?
cards.hint: Hint — only when the 'if' part really happens.
cards.coAnswer: Together: only when you touch the spike! That's the rule.
cards.mustHaveIdea: an action that happens only when a condition is true
cards.olderCheck: Make a rule for what should happen when your lives hit zero.

cards.teach: A bump is when two things touch — like your car hitting the ramp to jump!
cards.check: If the car drives right into the ramp, did it BUMP it or MISS it?
cards.reteach: Like when you high-five a friend — your hands touch. If the car and ramp touch, is that a bump or a miss?
cards.coAnswer: Together: that's a BUMP! Two things touching.
cards.olderCheck: What's a bump doing when your dino hits a rock?

cards.teach: X and Y are like the address of where your character is standing on the screen.
cards.check: Does X go ACROSS (left and right), or UP AND DOWN?
cards.reteach: Think of a treasure map: 'go 3 steps across, then 2 steps up.' The across part is X. So is X across, or up and down?
cards.hint: Hint — X is the sideways one.
cards.coAnswer: Together: X goes across! Y goes up and down.
cards.mustHaveIdea: a thing's position on the screen (across and up/down)
cards.olderCheck: How would you move your ship to the top-right corner?

cards.teach: A picture-that-moves is the drawing of your character that zooms around the screen.
cards.check: Is the picture-that-moves the BACKGROUND, or the THING that moves around?
cards.reteach: In a puppet show, the puppet moves but the painted wall behind it stays still. Which one is the picture-that-moves — the puppet, or the wall?
cards.hint: Hint — it's the part that actually moves.
cards.coAnswer: Together: the thing that moves! That's your sprite.
cards.mustHaveIdea: the moving graphic of a game character/object (not the background)
cards.olderCheck: What's the difference between the sprite and the background?

cards.teach: A game-sound plays when something happens — like a 'boing!' when you jump.
cards.check: When you grab a coin, should it go DING, or stay totally SILENT?
cards.reteach: Like a doorbell — it dings when someone presses it. Should grabbing a coin make a happy ding, or no sound at all?
cards.hint: Hint — a fun ding makes it feel great!
cards.coAnswer: Together: DING! A sound when you grab a coin.
cards.mustHaveIdea: a sound that plays tied to an event in the game
cards.olderCheck: When should the win sound play in your game?

cards.teach: A clock counts time so your game can be a race against the clock!
cards.check: Does the clock COUNT the time, or PAINT the background?
cards.reteach: Like the timer on a microwave counting down to zero. Does our clock count the seconds, or color in the background?
cards.coAnswer: Together: it COUNTS the time! Race on.
cards.olderCheck: How does the timer make your game harder?

cards.teach: The win/lose rule decides exactly when you WIN or when you LOSE the game.
cards.check: Do you WIN when you get 10 coins, or when you get 0 coins?
cards.reteach: In a race, you win when you cross the finish line — that's the win rule. For our game, do you win with lots of coins (10) or none (0)?
cards.coAnswer: Together: you win at 10 coins! That's the win rule.
cards.mustHaveIdea: the rule that decides when the game is won or lost
cards.olderCheck: Which is a better win rule for our game — and why?

cards.teach: Saving writes your stuff down so it's still here when you come back tomorrow.
cards.check: After we save your game, will your level be here tomorrow — yes or no?
cards.reteach: Like writing your name in your notebook so it's there the next school day. If we save, is your level here tomorrow?
cards.hint: Hint — saving keeps it for later!
cards.coAnswer: Together: YES! Saving keeps it for tomorrow.
cards.mustHaveIdea: keeps your progress after you close the game
cards.olderCheck: Why do we save the game?

cards.teach: Putting it online means your game goes on the internet so your friends can play it too!
cards.check: When a game is online, can ONLY YOU play it, or can FRIENDS play it too?
cards.reteach: Like putting a video on the internet — anybody with the link can watch. So if your game is online, is it just for you, or for friends too?
cards.hint: Hint — sharing means others can play!
cards.coAnswer: Together: friends can play too! That's putting it online.
cards.mustHaveIdea: makes the game playable by other people over the internet
cards.olderCheck: Why might we keep it on our computer first before putting it online?

cards.teach: A dice-roll lets the computer surprise you — so the game is a little different every time!
cards.check: Does a dice-roll make things the SAME every time, or DIFFERENT every time?
cards.reteach: Like rolling real dice in a board game — you never know what you'll get. Does that make each turn the same, or different?
cards.coAnswer: Together: DIFFERENT every time! That's the dice-roll.
cards.olderCheck: Where could we use a dice-roll to surprise the player?

cards.teach: A list holds lots of things together — like ALL the coins in your game in one place.
cards.check: Does a list hold just ONE thing, or MANY things?
cards.reteach: Like a grocery list with milk, eggs, and bread all on it. Does a list hold one item, or lots of items?
cards.coAnswer: Together: MANY things! That's a list.
cards.mustHaveIdea: holds many items together in one place
cards.olderCheck: Why use a list for all the asteroids instead of one each?

cards.teach: The game-mode is what the game is doing right now — PLAYING, PAUSED, or GAME OVER.
cards.check: Right now while you're playing, is the game-mode PLAYING or PAUSED?
cards.reteach: Like a TV that can be ON, OFF, or on PAUSE. While you're actually playing, which mode is the game in — playing or paused?
cards.mustHaveIdea: the current mode/state the game is in
cards.olderCheck: Why do we need a separate 'game over' mode from 'playing'?
